_notes: origin 'original': Phronesis analysis, full body stored here
origin 'via': curated from law reviews or legal blogs — abstract only (open-access) or preview (paywalled)
analysis_type: doctrinal | case | statutory | regulatory | procedural | litigation | policy | comparative

entries.title: Section 230 After NetChoice: What the Court Left Unsaid
entries.subtitle: A close reading of the 2024 NetChoice opinions, the doctrinal questions they resolved, and the larger questions they deliberately avoided.
entries.abstract: The Supreme Court's 2024 NetChoice opinions were widely anticipated as a landmark statement on platform speech. This analysis examines what the Court actually held — as opposed to what it was asked to decide — and maps the doctrinal questions left open for lower courts, Congress, and the next wave of platform litigation.

entries.title: Compelled Speech and Algorithmic Curation After Moody v. NetChoice
entries.subtitle: Examines whether must-carry and must-not-carry obligations on platforms constitute compelled speech under the First Amendment as clarified by Moody.
entries.abstract: State statutes requiring platforms to carry certain content — or prohibiting them from removing it — raise compelled speech questions that the Court's NetChoice opinions addressed only obliquely. This analysis applies the Court's reasoning to the must-carry/must-not-carry distinction and assesses the constitutional viability of content moderation mandates.

entries.title: FTC v. Amazon: What the Complaint Reveals About Modern Monopolization Theory
entries.subtitle: An analysis of the FTC's Amazon complaint as a window into how enforcement agencies are attempting to modernize Sherman Act Section 2 doctrine.
entries.court: U.S. District Court for the Western District of Washington
entries.abstract: The FTC's 2023 complaint against Amazon marks an ambitious attempt to apply Section 2 of the Sherman Act to a platform ecosystem rather than a discrete market. This analysis reads the complaint as a doctrinal argument — examining the market definition choices, the conduct theories, and the remedial logic — and assesses their prospects under existing case law.

entries.title: Loper Bright and the Future of FCC Rulemaking Authority
entries.subtitle: Traces the implications of the Court's Chevron overruling for the FCC's ability to promulgate broadband and platform regulations.
entries.abstract: The Supreme Court's overruling of Chevron deference in Loper Bright has immediate consequences for agencies that have long relied on their own statutory interpretations to justify expansive rulemaking. This analysis focuses on the FCC, mapping the regulations most vulnerable to post-Loper Bright challenge and considering what statutory revision would be required to restore the agency's authority.

entries.title: Copyright and the Training Data Question: Where Courts Are Headed
entries.subtitle: Surveys pending litigation over AI training data and sketches the likely doctrinal paths courts will take to resolve the fair use question.
entries.abstract: A wave of copyright litigation targeting AI training practices is working its way through the federal courts. This analysis surveys the pending cases, maps the competing fair use arguments, and sketches the doctrinal paths most likely to produce a stable resolution — including the possibility that Congress will need to act regardless of how courts rule.
